title: OASA Telematics Redesign
engagement: National and Kapodistrian University of Athens
summary: I redesigned OASA Telematics around the question a passenger actually has at a stop: should I wait, walk, or take another route? The work moved from user research and task analysis to a tested Android prototype in Figma.
problem: The original app had the data, but getting to an answer still meant moving between arrivals, vehicle positions, route details, and announcements. The redesign had to bring those clues into one decision path on a small screen, often checked outdoors and in a hurry.

cover.alt: OASA Telematics mobile app redesign shown on a phone beside the project title
cover.caption: OASA Telematics mobile app redesign

context.heading: An independent redesign of a public-service app
context.body: This was my MSc dissertation at the National and Kapodistrian University of Athens, not a project commissioned by OASA. I treated the app as a service used by daily commuters, occasional riders, older passengers, and people with accessibility needs. The scope covered the Android experience from first entry to live journey updates.

research.heading: The research pointed to decisions, not more features
research.body: Arrivals were the most-used part of the existing app, followed by vehicle positions. People wanted stronger live tracking, clearer maps, visible service notices, fewer taps, and a reliable way to use core information when connectivity drops. A six-person focus group added the practical details: show nearby stops, keep arrivals prominent, remember recent trips, and reduce the amount of information competing on each screen.

stats.label: people responded to the initial transport-app survey
wanted an offline mode for essential journey information
asked for better live bus and trolley tracking

structure.heading: Less navigation, more continuity
structure.body: The answer was not to keep adding destinations. In fact, 52% of survey participants felt the existing app already had the right number of functions. I used scenarios, hierarchical task analysis, essential use cases, and a closed card sort with ten participants to reorganise those functions around five jobs: plan a trip, locate a vehicle, inspect a route, manage tickets, and control personal settings. Low-fidelity flows in Whimsical came first; the high-fidelity system was then built and linked in Figma.

boards.alt: Figma canvas showing authentication and route-planning screens for OASA Telematics
boards.caption: Authentication and journey-planning flows

boards.alt: Figma canvas showing ticket, profile, settings, and support screens for OASA Telematics
boards.caption: Tickets, profile, settings, and support

groups.heading: Enter the app on your terms
groups.body: First use has three paths: sign in, create an account, or continue as a guest when an arrival time matters more than registration. The account branch keeps the work in a short sequence: enter the essential details, decide whether to add a profile photo, then move straight into journey planning.

groups.heading: Plan a trip, then stay with it
groups.body: This is the main product flow from the dissertation. The map stays present while the passenger moves from a nearby starting point to destination search, departure comparison, route selection, step-by-step directions, nearby service, live vehicle tracking, stop arrivals, and service updates. It is one continuous decision path rather than a set of disconnected map tools.

screens.caption: Start with what is nearby

groups.heading: Keep tickets in the travel context
groups.body: The ticket flow gives ATH.ENA Card information its own place in the main navigation, beside routes rather than inside settings. The available Figma export captures the overview state, where a passenger can check the card and move into fare information without leaving the transport app.

screens.alt: Tickets overview with ATH.ENA Card information and fare options
screens.caption: Open the ATH.ENA Card overview

groups.heading: Carry familiar journeys into the profile
groups.body: The profile is not just an account record. It brings favourite routes and recent journeys together with personal details, then provides a focused edit state for the information the passenger can change. That keeps repeat travel useful without crowding the planner itself.

screens.alt: Profile overview with personal information, favourite routes, and recent journeys
screens.caption: Review favourites and history

screens.alt: Profile editing screen for photo, username, full name, and phone number
screens.caption: Edit personal details

groups.heading: Make settings part of the service
groups.body: The settings flow covers more than preferences. It introduces the app, manages permissions, supports accessible use, publishes service news, opens a direct contact route, and keeps privacy and app information readable. Showing the whole sequence matters because these are the screens that make a public-service product understandable and accountable.

screens.alt: Settings overview with permissions, accessibility, route notifications, privacy, news, and app information
screens.caption: Find settings by purpose

screens.alt: App introduction screen explaining how OASA Telematics supports journey planning

screens.caption: Control app permissions

screens.alt: Accessibility controls for text size, contrast, screen reader, text to speech, and external devices
screens.caption: Adjust the interface

screens.alt: OASA Telematics news and announcements screen with service articles
screens.caption: Read service news

screens.caption: Reach support directly

screens.caption: Check app information

evaluation.heading: Testing changed the interface, not just the report
evaluation.body: The interactive Figma prototype was evaluated with WAMMI, think-aloud sessions, and a heuristic review. The 91.17% WAMMI score describes perceived usability of the prototype in this study, not performance of a live product. More useful than the headline score were the points of hesitation: unclear time controls, weak distinction between departure and arrival, buried nearby stops, and an account gate that slowed down first use.

stats.label: global WAMMI usability score for the prototype
experts reviewed the prototype against usability heuristics

changes: Added guest access and moved the origin-destination swap control beside the fields.
Labelled alternate departure time instead of relying on an unfamiliar icon.
Made the current stop and nearby stops visible in the journey context.
Grouped notifications and gave unread service updates a clearer state.
Renamed saved routes as favourites and replaced the ambiguous icon with a heart.
Added voice-notification options and a direct route to support.

output.heading: A complete prototype, with a clear centre of gravity
output.body: The final Android prototype covers onboarding, registration, trip planning, live vehicle and stop tracking, service notifications, tickets, profile, permissions, accessibility, and support. The breadth matters, but the central idea is simple: keep the passenger oriented as the question changes from where can I go, to what should I take, to where is it now.
